questions.question: Describe the iterative approach to reversing a singly linked list without creating new nodes. What are the three main pointers used and what is their role in the process?
keyPoints: The 'current' pointer traverses the list, the 'next' pointer temporarily stores the next node to avoid losing the reference, and the 'previous' pointer trails behind to allow the 'current' node to point backwards.
The algorithm iterates through the list, reversing the 'next' pointer of the 'current' node to point to the 'previous' node, effectively changing the direction of the links.

questions.question: Explain the base case and the recursive step in a recursive algorithm designed to reverse a singly linked list in-place.
keyPoints: The base case is when the function receives a null 'head' or a list with only one node; in this case, it simply returns the head as the new head of the reversed list.
The recursive step involves calling the function on the 'next' node of the current head, which will eventually return the new head of the reversed list.

After the recursive call returns, the algorithm adjusts the pointers so that the next node of the current head's successor now points back to the current head, and the current head's 'next' pointer is set to null to break the original link.

questions.question: Why is it crucial to temporarily store the 'next' pointer before changing the 'current.next' link during the iterative reversal of a linked list?
keyPoints: If the 'next' pointer is not stored, the reference to the rest of the list is lost once the 'current.next' link is redirected to the 'previous' node.
Losing this reference would make it impossible to continue traversing the list, effectively breaking the algorithm and potentially causing a loss of data.
